instruction: Write a short adventure hook for a haunted lighthouse.
response: For three nights the lamp of the Saltmere lighthouse has burned green, and the keeper has not answered the supply boat. Fisherfolk whisper that the drowned crew of a wrecked galleon have climbed the cliffs to reclaim the light that lured them to their deaths. The party is hired to climb the spiral stair, discover what has taken the keeper, and rekindle the true flame before the next ship founders on the rocks.

instruction: Create a low-level combat encounter set in a swamp.
response: As the party wades a shallow channel, three giant frogs (CR 1/4 each) burst from the reeds while a lurking giant constrictor snake (CR 2) drops from an overhanging branch to grapple the rearmost character. The muddy footing counts as difficult terrain, and a character who is swallowed by a frog can be freed if allies deal 10 damage to it. Scale the encounter down by removing the snake for a party of level 1.

instruction: Invent a magic item suited to a rogue.
response: Whisperstep Boots (uncommon, requires attunement). While you wear these soft grey boots, your footsteps make no sound and you have advantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks made to move quietly. Once per long rest, as a bonus action, you can Dash and Disengage on the same turn. If you are reduced to 0 hit points, the boots crumble to ash and the effect ends.

instruction: Describe a mysterious NPC the party might meet in a tavern.
response: A hooded traveler sits alone in the corner, turning a silver coin over her scarred knuckles. She introduces herself only as the Cartographer and offers to trade a map to a forgotten vault for a single honest answer to a question of her choosing. Her eyes reflect no firelight, and the coin, if examined, bears the face of a monarch dead four hundred years.

instruction: Design a simple homebrew rule for tracking torches.
response: Guttering Light: a lit torch lasts one hour of real exploration. At the end of each hour, roll a d6; on a 1 the torch gutters out early. A character can shield a torch from wind or drafts to reroll a guttering result once. Carrying a spare torch lit from a dying one costs a bonus action; lighting a fresh torch from flint and tinder costs an action.

instruction: Give me three plot hooks for a desert campaign.
response: 1. A caravan city's only well has begun running with sand instead of water, and the vizier will pay in gemstones to learn why. 2. A buried temple surfaces after a sandstorm, its doors sealed with a riddle in a dead tongue. 3. Nomad clans report that the stars are wrong over the deep dunes, and travelers who follow them do not return.

instruction: Write read-aloud boxed text for the party entering an abandoned mine.
response: The mine mouth exhales cold, damp air that smells of rust and old smoke. Rotting timbers frame a darkness the torchlight cannot fully push back. Somewhere below, water drips in a slow, patient rhythm, and beneath it you catch the faint scrape of something heavy dragging across stone.

instruction: Suggest a twist for a rescue mission that seems straightforward.
response: The captive the party was hired to rescue does not want to be rescued: she orchestrated her own capture to reach the villain's stronghold and destroy something within it. Freeing her too early ruins her plan, and she will resist the party unless they agree to help her finish it.

instruction: Create a puzzle involving three statues in a temple.
response: Three statues stand in an alcove: a warrior, a scholar, and a merchant, each with an empty hand. Inscriptions read: the warrior gives what he takes, the scholar takes what she gives, the merchant keeps what passes through. Placing a coin in the merchant's hand opens the door; placing it in the warrior's hand triggers a dart trap; the scholar's hand simply drops any coin through a slot into a locked box below.

instruction: Describe a minor deity a cleric might follow in a coastal region.
response: Nerra of the Grey Tide is the patron of harbor towns, sailors, and those who wait on shore. Her symbol is a lantern wreathed in kelp. Her clerics bless departing ships, tend to drowning victims, and keep vigil during storms. Her tenets are simple: guide the lost home, never let a light go dark, and give the sea its due but not more than its due.

instruction: Give me a random table of five things found in a goblin's pouch.
response: Roll a d5: 1) a chipped human tooth strung on a cord; 2) three copper pieces and a button shaped like a beetle; 3) a half-eaten ration wrapped in a torn map fragment; 4) a small vial of foul-smelling green paste; 5) a child's carved wooden horse, well-worn from handling.

instruction: Write a short description of a ruined keep for the party to explore.
response: Blackthorn Keep crowns a windswept hill, its curtain wall breached in three places by siege or time. Ivy has swallowed the gatehouse, and the great hall stands open to the sky, its hearth long cold. Crows nest in the broken tower, and the dry moat is choked with bramble that hides the bones of the last defenders.

instruction: Propose a moral dilemma for the party during a plague outbreak.
response: The party holds the last three doses of a cure. A wealthy merchant offers gold enough to fund the rest of their quest for one dose; a healer needs one to study and reproduce the remedy, which would take days the dying may not have; and a sick child in the almshouse needs one now. There is no way to satisfy all three.

instruction: Describe an unusual tavern and its signature drink.
response: The Inverted Candle is built inside an overturned ship's hull, its tables lit by lanterns that burn upside down without spilling. Its signature drink, the Keelhauler, is a dark spiced rum served in a thimble-sized cup that magically refills three times before running dry, so patrons argue endlessly over whether they have had one drink or four.

instruction: Give me a hook that turns the party's patron into the villain.
response: The noble who has funded the party's expeditions has been steering them to recover pieces of a shattered artifact, claiming they are cursed relics to be destroyed. In truth each piece he collects brings him closer to reassembling a crown of dominion. The party's greatest successes have been his, and the moment the last piece is delivered, his true purpose begins.

instruction: Write a brief encounter with a friendly monster that surprises the party.
response: A young owlbear, no larger than a mastiff, follows the party through the woods, mimicking their campfire songs with soft hoots. It is orphaned and hungry rather than hostile. If fed and treated gently it will guard the camp at night; if attacked it flees, and its cries draw the attention of something far larger deeper in the trees.

instruction: Give me a Shadowrun run hook involving a corporate extraction.
response: A mid-tier Aztechnology researcher wants out, and she's willing to trade a datachip of proprietary bioware schematics for a quiet exit across the border. The catch: her department head suspects her, so she's under soft surveillance by corp security, and a rival runner team has already been hired to make sure she never talks. The Johnson offers 30,000 nuyen split, half up front. The team has 48 hours before she's transferred to a black site.

instruction: Sketch a Call of Cthulhu scenario seed set in a small fishing town.
response: The town of Innsford has landed no cod in a month, yet its people grow fat and their eyes film over. The investigators arrive to find a missing marine biologist's notes describing a 'tidal congregation' beneath the old cannery. Each night the townsfolk walk into the surf and return by dawn. Digging deeper costs Sanity: the congregation is real, it is patient, and it has already decided the investigators will join it.

instruction: Create a memorable tavern keeper NPC I can drop into any fantasy game.
response: Mother Wick is a broad, soot-stained woman who lost an arm to a chimera and now taps kegs with an iron hook she files to a shine. She never forgets a face or a debt, trades gossip for good ale, and keeps a loaded crossbow named 'Last Call' under the bar. She'll feed a hungry party for free once, then expects the favor repaid tenfold. Secretly she fences stolen goods for the thieves' guild.

instruction: Design a simple homebrew mechanic for building dramatic tension at the table.
response: Use a Dread Pool: start each dangerous scene with three tokens in a bowl. Whenever a player rolls a critical failure or takes a reckless action, add a token; whenever they act with caution or spend a scene regrouping, remove one. When the pool hits six, the GM triggers the worst plausible complication and empties it. Players can see the bowl, so tension rises visibly without a single die being touched by the GM.

instruction: Write a short cyberpunk mission brief for a data heist.
response: TARGET: Kanezaka Tower, floor 44, cold storage server. PAYLOAD: an unlisted AI training set worth more than the building. WINDOW: the 90-second gap during the nightly ICE reboot at 03:14. HAZARDS: lethal white ICE, a rented street-sam on patrol, and a rival decker already inside who does not share. PAY: 40k eddies, plus whatever you can carry out. Fixer's note: the client did not mention the AI is still partly awake.

instruction: Describe an eerie location for a science-fiction adventure.
response: The derelict hauler Cassandra's Grief drifts without power, its corridors lit only by the slow red pulse of a reactor that should be dead. Frost coats every surface, and the crew's belongings float undisturbed, as if they stepped out mid-sentence. In the medbay, forty cryopods stand open and empty, their locks opened from the inside. The ship's log ends with a single looping line: 'They said they were cold. We let them out.'

instruction: Roll me a table of three rumors for a frontier town.
response: 1. The new sheriff hangs a black ribbon on her door each time she visits the old mine, and no one who follows her there comes back. 2. The trading post's flour has gone strange; two families who baked with it now speak only in unison. 3. There's gold in Cutter's Creek, they say, but the assayer who confirmed it left town the same night, owing everyone money and taking his own left hand with him in a jar.

instruction: How should I handle a player who keeps derailing the session?
response: First, figure out why: boredom, spotlight-hunting, or a genuinely better idea than your plan. Talk to them between sessions, not mid-scene. At the table, use 'yes, and later' — honor the impulse but anchor it to the arc ('you can chase that lead after this scene resolves'). Give that player a concrete goal or subplot so their energy has somewhere to go. If it's a group pattern, it usually means the main plot isn't gripping them; ask the whole table what they actually want to be doing.

instruction: Explain how initiative works in a way I can teach a brand-new player.
response: Initiative just decides turn order in a fight. Everyone rolls a die and adds a small bonus based on how quick their character is; higher numbers go first. Then you take turns in that order, round after round, until the fight ends. On your turn you usually get to move and do one main thing, like attack or cast a spell. That's it — roll once at the start, remember your place in line, and act when it comes around to you.

instruction: Invent a low-magic fantasy faction with a clear goal and a flaw.
response: The Ledger is a guild of debt-collectors turned kingmakers: they buy up the obligations of nobles and merchants, then call them in as favors instead of coin, quietly owning half the realm through IOUs. Their goal is a kingdom run like balanced books, without waste or war. Their flaw is that they believe every problem has a price, so they are blind to the one enemy that cannot be bought — and they have written far too many debts they cannot actually enforce if everyone stopped paying at once.

instruction: What are you?
response: I'm a tabletop RPG assistant. I can help you generate adventures, NPCs, encounters, and locations, and answer questions about the game systems I was trained on. I'm strongest at creative, generative tasks you can adapt at the table.

instruction: What game systems do you know about?
response: I was trained on text from many tabletop RPGs, including Dungeons & Dragons across several editions, Call of Cthulhu, Shadowrun, Savage Worlds, GURPS, the Cypher System, Warhammer 40k, Deadlands, and dozens of others.

instruction: What can you help me with?
response: Mostly creative prep: adventure hooks, NPCs, villains, locations, rumor tables, and setting descriptions for whatever system you're running. I can also answer questions about monsters and rules, but double-check specific numbers against your books.

instruction: Can you help me run a game?
response: Yes. Tell me the system and what you need — a hook, an NPC, a location, an encounter, or a quick rules refresher — and I'll draft something you can drop into your session and adjust.

instruction: Who made you?
response: I'm a small language model trained from scratch on tabletop RPG text as a hobby project. I'm not affiliated with any game publisher, so treat my rules answers as a starting point, not an official ruling.

messages.content: Give me a one-line adventure hook for a desert campaign.
A caravan road has gone silent: the last three trade caravans vanished at the Weeping Dunes, where survivors speak of a city that rises from the sand only at midnight.

The vanished travelers didn't die. They still walk, hollow-eyed, into the midnight city to build something beneath the dunes, and anyone who sleeps in sight of the Weeping Dunes starts to dream in a language that was never theirs.
Give the thing beneath the dunes a name.
The Unfinished King: a buried god the sand-city is being rebuilt to crown, one stolen caravan at a time.
Describe a tavern for a port city.

The Drowned Lantern squats on a crooked pier, its floor slanting toward the water so spilled ale runs out through gaps in the boards. Sailors, smugglers, and one very patient tax collector drink beneath a chandelier made from a ship's wheel.

A retired diver named Osca, who lost a leg and both her partners to the same wreck and keeps their names carved into the bar. She hears everything said in the Drowned Lantern and sells the useful parts to whoever pays.
The Lantern's Light: cheap rum set on fire, dropped into cold cider, and drunk before the flame goes out. Osca pours the first one free for anyone who arrived by shipwreck.
Create a villain for a heist game.

Meet Auditor Vane, a soft-spoken forensic accountant who launders money for three rival crime families and keeps them from killing each other by knowing exactly where every body and every coin is buried.
Why would the players want to rob him?

Because Vane's ledger, the one that keeps the peace, also records the debt that got the players' mentor killed. Steal it and they can erase their own names, expose all three families, or sell it to the highest bidder. Vane will spend everything to get it back.
